symptom:
WMI functions fail on the Windows Server. Wbemtest returns 0x800706ba 'The RPC server is unavailable' when connect to target
namespace root\cimv2.  In Inbound Rules under Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security both Distributed Transaction Coordinator(RPC)/Windows Management Instrumentation have been enabled.  Port 135 is reachable on the target.
rootCause:
Rule merging is disabled and RPC high dynamic port on the target machine has been blocked by domain policy. Though those ports been allowed in the local firewall inbound rules. Since rule merging is disabled, the machine will apply domain policy.   The effectiveness of local firewall rules depends on whether
rule merging is enabled. 
 If rule merging is
     enabled:
     Local firewall rules will apply along with domain firewall rules. 
 If rule merging is
     disabled:
     Only domain firewall rules will apply, and local firewall rules will be
     ignored.  This setting controls whether locally defined rules can
supplement or override domain-defined rules.
solution: Configured inbound rules in the domain policy to allow all high dynamic ports.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#197280

symptom: Abstract On all DNS servers the customer noticed a flood of informational event with ID 5504.   The event contains the following info  The DNS server encountered an invalid domain name in a packet from #IP Address. The packet will be rejected. The event data contains the DNS packet. We collected network traces while the event got recoded to check further
rootCause: Upon checking the traces, the following behavior can be spotted in the DNSSRV etl trace  [0] 0080.37C0::12/12/22-09:19:46.6740753 Answer type 1 does not match question type 65,    nor is possible answer for this question    Tossing response packet 00000139F994BCC0 for orginal query at 00000139FA225CD0[0]0080.37C0::12/12/22-09:19:46.6740876 The DNS server encountered an invalid domain name in a packet from 165.225.73.70. The packet will be rejected. The event data contains the DNS packet.  192.168.1.50, 79[0] 0080.37C0::12/12/22-09:19:46.6740880 Reporting EVENT 0x00001580 5504 error code 0 From packet tracing, it seems the client send the query to the DNS server with query type 65 ( HTTPs) Domain Name System (response)    Transaction ID: 0x9ce7    Flags: 0x8400 Standard query response, No error    Questions: 1    Answer RRs: 1    Authority RRs: 1    Additional RRs: 1    Queries        pac.zscloud.net: type HTTPS, class IN            Name: pac.zscalar.net            [Name Length: 15]            [Label Count: 3]            Type: HTTPS (HTTPS Specific Service Endpoints) (65)            Class: IN (0x0001) The DNS server forwarded the query to Zscalar server which is configured as the forwarder for the CORP. Zscalar responded with answer that has another query type ( Type1 , host A)  Answers        pac.zscloud.net: type A, class IN, addr 192.168.1.50            Name: pac.zscalar.net            Type: A (Host Address) (1)            Class: IN (0x0001)            Time to live: 120 (2 minutes)            Data length: 4 The DNS server reject this packet and record the event 5504 since the answer does not match the query.
solution: Since the initial query was sent from Zscalar client to resolve PAC file from Zscalar server, the DNS administrator was this advised to discuss issue with the vendor to either correct the query sent by Zscalar client or fix the response coming from Zscalar server.  There was no business impact as the client was sending two queries for host A and HTTPs but the Zscalar server was returning only Host A type for both.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#197635

symptom:
The reported issue is
that the MBAM client is not reporting in the MBAM Console.
rootCause:
Incorrect
     URL settings for MBAM recovery service endpoint and MBAM status reporting
     service endpoint in Group Policy settings.
solution: Correct URL Settings: We checked and found that the MBAM recovery service endpoint and MBAM status reporting service endpoint URLs were wrongly set in Group Policy settings. Corrected the URLs and ran gpupdate /force on the client machine.  Enable MBAM Status Reporting Service: Noticed that the MBAM status reporting service was set to disabled. Enabled the service and ran gpupdate /force.  Firewall Settings: Turned off the firewall on the client system.  Refresh IIS Server: Refreshed the Microsoft BitLocker Administration and Monitoring reports in IIS server. After refreshing, the client system started reporting correctly in the client compliance status.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#202558

symptom: Abstract This article describes circumstances under which devices implementing MIT Kerberos libraries will fail authentication when used in conjunction with a Windows 2008 or 2008R2 domain which utilizes Read Only Domain Controllers (RODC). This article describes the symptoms and configurations under which the issue may present itself and suggests potential workarounds. In addition, it explains the underlying reason for the failure. Symptoms In an environment with one or more RODCs authentication may fail when interacting with certain MIT based Kerberos devices in one of the following scenarios. MIT Kerberos may be used by several variants of the Unix and Linux operating systems. The MIT Kerberos component is also used on common Filer solutions. The client is an MIT device which received a TGT from Windows KDC on RODC The client passes a TGT generated by Windows KDC on RODC to MIT Device which in turn uses the TGT to request a TGS on behalf of the calling user.   In both scenarios the TGT will have been issued by an RODC where the msDS-SecondaryKrbTgtNumber associated with the krbtgt account for that RODC will have a value greater than 32767. In the first scenario the client will receive the error KRB_ERR_S_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN in response to any TGS request. In the second scenario the TGS request will be silently dropped by the Windows Key Distribution Center KDC and a TCP connection Reset response will be issued. In this circumstance capturing the traffic from the device you will see that the Key Version Number (KVNO) in the outgoing request is prepended with a 0, making the entire KVNO 5- bytes in length. Windows KDCs will always reject a 5-byte KVNO which is evaluated as malformed. Note: Other clients may be unaffected and depending upon network topology the issue may be intermittent on affected clients
rootCause: The issue occurs because of a difference in the way the Microsoft and MIT Kerberos standards encode and interpret the KVNO value. In a Microsoft Kerberos environment and when using RODCs the 4 bytes of KVNO are actually interpreted as two 2-byte values. For example, the decimal KVNO 2870149121 in hex is AB 13 00 01. The first pair of octets represent the RODCs unique ID and the second pair represent the version number of the key. Together these uniquely identify to the KDC which principal and secret were used to create the key. In an MIT based Kerberos implementation the KVNO is treated as a single 32-bit value, however this in itself does not cause an issue. Crucially the encoding of the value differs between Microsoft and MIT Kerberos implementations, MIT Kerberos encodes and decodes the entire KVNO as a 32bit value using ASN.1 DER rules whereas the Windows KDC decodes only the lower 16bits of the KVNO as an ASN.1 DER integer and accepts the upper 16 bits as the RODCID without decoding. Due to the differences in the encoding/decoding behavior an MIT based Kerberos client will prepend a 0 to the KVNO value. This is because it encodes the entire KVNO strictly as an ASN.1 DER integer using twos-complement notation. Using this notation the high bit of a value is deducted from the sum of the preceding bits to derive the encoded value. Therefore, the high bit of the encoded value MUST always be 0 if it is to be decoded as a positive or unsigned integer. This means that when the 16th bit of an RODC Unique ID is 1, i.e. when the RODC ID is 0x8000 (decimal 32768) or greater, it will cause an MIT implementation to prepend a 0 and thus generate a 5-byte KVNO.
solution: There are 2 options to mitigate this issuePrevent users and services accessing MIT Kerberos clients from authenticating via an RODC. This can be done by denying the RODC the ability to replicate the password for the account or group of accounts via the Password Replication Policy.OR Pre-create the RODC account discarding any that have RODC unique IDs over 32768

symptom:
Abstract  Windows Updates released on and after 2021 4B contain protections for an elevation of privilege vulnerability tracked by CVE-2021-28316 where an attacker with physical access can trigger a user-logon locked, domain-joined device with Wi-Fi network connectivity to authenticate against a rogue access point in order to capture an MSCHAPV2 challenge-response hash (Machine Authentication) for the domain computer account. This challenge-response hash can be submitted to crack.sh along with the domain SID and a service name to recover the NTLM hash of the domain computer account in less than 24 hours (as of April 2021). The attack fully bypasses BitLocker disk encryption and local administrator permissions on the device.  By default, domain joined Windows workstations have allowed access to the network selection UI from the lock screen.  Windows Updates released on and after 2024 8B released August 13, 2024 contain additional protections for CVE-2021-28316 that intentionally disable the "Use my Windows User Account" checkbox on domain joined devices with an active wireless network connection. Specifically, when attempting the unlock a locked Windows computer configured with an active Wi-Fi-connection, the "Use my Windows user account" checkbox is intentionally disabled or greyed out.  The August 13, 2024 Windows Update KBs introducing this change contain the following release note text:  This update addresses CVE-2021-28316. Because of this, the "Use my Windows user account" checkbox is NOT available on the lock screen to connect to Wi-Fi.  Status Updates  August 8, 2024: KB created in anticipation of very intentional, security-focused cheese moving behavior change in Windows Updates released on and after August 13, 2024. Changes in Task Group 48993011 appear to be covered by a KIR.    Resources  CVE-2021-28316: Windows WLAN AutoConfig Service Security Feature Bypass Vulnerability ShenanigansLab: Airstrike Attack - FDE bypass and EoP on domain joined Windows workstations (CVE-2021-28316) Full play-by-play analysis of showing how to obtain the NTLM hash of a domain computer account with EOP to local admin on a pre-2021 4B-patched Windows 10 computer.    Symptoms The "Use my Windows user account" checkbox cannot be enabled on -8b.24-patched domain joined Windows clients and servers
  The "Use my Windows user account" checkbox could be enabled on pre-8b.24-patched domain joined Windows clients and servers 
  
  The "use my Windows user account" checkbox is intentionally disabled / unchecked on when attempting to unlock a locked user logon session on an 8b.24-patched, domain joined Windows clients and server with an active Wi-Fi connection.

Customers may configure Windows to use EAP-MS-CHAPv2 Authentication.  What is the full impact of enabling the User my Windows User account checkbox in the Airstrike Attack-FDE bypass tracked by CVE-2021-28316 and the corresponding EOPA2:>Prior to 2021 4B, an attacker with physical access to a locked device with Wi-Fi capabilities (such as a laptop) could force the laptop to authenticate against a rogue access point to capture a MSCHAPv2 challenge response hash for the domain computer account.. Due to known Cryptographic weakness in the MSCHAPv2 Protocol, the MSCHAPv2 challenge response hash can then be submitted to any external website to recover the NTLM hash. The fix in 2021 4B that mitigated CVE-2021-85654 confirmed by the security finder stopped attempting machine authentication for new connections initiated by non-interactive users (Logon UI).Prior to the release 2024 8B, enabling the "Use my Windows user account checkbox" while connecting to a roque AP allows the finder to capture the MSCHAPv2 challenge response hash. That vulnerability is mitigated by disabling the checkbox in Windows Updates released on or after 2024 8B.  Is the "Use my Windows user account checkbox always disabled when attempting to unlock a locked user session on a domain-joined, 8B-patched Windows device with an active Wifi-Connection. A3. symptom: Abstract  As part of the security hardening for CVE-2024-37968, the DNS server on Windows Server will now validate the 1st use of glue records. The DNS Client queries may fail when the glue records for the domains are out-of-date. Prior to installing Windows Updates released on or after August 13, 2024, customers should ensure that the glue records registered on parent domain are valid and match the data provided by the authoritative name servers. Any out-of-date IP addresses of the glue records should be removed or updated to prevent DNS client queries from failing. This KB contains the textual version of Test-DnsZoneNameNSRecord.ps1that makes a "best effort" to validate IP addresses for NS records in (1.) a single, admin specified DNS zone or (2.) All zones hosted by a Windows DNS Server.  Resources Internal KB5041440: Servicing: 8B.24: Caching and using glue records without validation may result in increased query processing or DNS query timeouts. Documents DNS hardening changes driven by Windows Updates released on or after August 13, 2024 contain protections for CVE-2024-37968 which trigger Windows DNS Servers to validate glue records returned by a parent domain prior to their 1st use.    ROE Test-DnsZoneNameNSRecord.ps1 NOT supported by Microsoft, Microsoft developers or Microsoft Support. Test-DnsZoneNameNSRecord.ps1 is an unsupported code sample that makes a best effort to find common configuration errors in NS records that may cause DNS queries to fail prior to or after installing Windows Updates released on or after August 13, 2024. DNS admins are responsible for validating the tool and making it production ready for their environments.  Test-DnsZoneNameNSRecord.ps1 is intended for internal use by the customer Microsoft initially shared it with during the context of an assisted support case or Microsoft support contract. Customers should NOT share original or derivative versions of Test-DnsZoneNameNSRecord.ps1 with other organizations. Similarly, such code samples should not be (2.) "published" on the internet or (3.) monetized in any way.   Test-DnsZoneNameNSRecord.ps1 may experience compatibility / interop issues on Microsoft DNS Servers older than Windows Server 2016 unless PowerShell version 5.1 is installed.   Execution  Pseudocode overview: Test-DnsZoneNameNSRecord.ps1 is a read-only code sample that (1.) discovers DNS zones (2.) performs PowerShell-based DNS queries against NS records in those zones (3.) compares values then (4.) reports on configurations that may DNS queries to fail on 8B.24-patched DNS Servers.   Test-DnsZoneNameNSRecord.ps1 does NOT make changes to the environment and send telemetry to any entity.   See the Help comments for usage details including how to (1.) remotely validate a single, admin-specified zone from a remote computer or (2.) ALL DNS zones hosted by a Microsoft DNS Server when Test-DnsZoneNameNSRecord.ps1 is run from the console of the DNS Server.  For questions NOT addressed by this article, case owners may send email  To: DNSHardeningIn8B24@microsoft.com  Subject: <case #> is a match for <this kb>: <Detailed subject line detailing what you need to unblock your customer> \ <company name> \ <case owner alias><Detailed problem statement with screenshots or text output.>

symptom: Abstract  On December 5, 2024, security finders at 0patch published a blog post identifying a zero-day vulnerability that allows an attacker to obtain a user's NTLM credentials by having the user view a malicious file downloaded from the attacker's web page in Windows Explorer The attack requires persuading a user to (1.) download a malicious file containing a crafted .url file onto their machine. (2.) Opening the folder triggers shell to iteratively call CInternetShortcut code over multiple paths in search of the correct icon for the URL file. If all paths fail, a default .url icon is used.  In the process of finding an icon, one of the methods used by .url files is to create a CShellLink object with the same target and then have it try to resolve its icon. The 1st attempt tries CShellLink::GetIconLocation using this object. Such attempts fail safely but subequently try CShellLink::Extract. In the process of calling Extract, shell pings the target location to make sure it is available before moving on with the icon extraction. This ping leaks the NTLM hash. Specifically, the ping solicits an NTLM challenge response to (5.) a malicious server controlled by the attacker (6.) to run a dictionary attack against the collected NTLM hash which was (7.) created using random data using the password as a key as opposed to a hash of the password. The attack leverages standard SSO workflow from a Windows perspective and is not inherently a security issue, aside from leveraging NTLM.  As of December 10, 2024, issue status is as follows:   This vulnerability has been reported to Microsoft  0patch published a public facing blog post announcing the existence of the vulnerability one day after notifying Microsoft.  0patch has intentionally NOT released a proof of concept (POC). 0patch is NOT aware of any threat actors exploiting this issue. 0patch has released a micropatch which appears to "hook" key Microsoft kernel binaries.  No CVE has been filed on behalf of this issue as of December 10, 2024.  Microsoft WSD, Feature and MSRC teams are actively investigating the specifics of this attack. This investigation will take time and will be represented in this KB once available.  Microsoft published "Mitigating NTLM Relay Attacks by Default" on MSRC blog.   Status Updates January 29, 2025: Bug table in resolution updated with bug numbers, resolving KBs and release dates tentatively targeted for release on 2B.25 / Feb 11, 2025 December 10, 2024: Link added to "talking points" + "Mitigating NTLM Relay Attacks by Default" on MSRC blog. KB article title changed. Minor word smithing changes.  December 9, 2024. KB created. ICM filed. Related cases identified.    Resources blog.0patch.com: URL File NTLM Hash Disclosure Vulnerability (0day) - and Free Micropatches for it Blog post posted December 5, 2024 by 0patch  MSRC: Mitigating NTLM Relay Attacks by Default Focus on Introduction, Background, Enabling NTLM Relay mitigations, AD CS and LDAP and Looking ahead: The future of NTLM sections of MSRC blog published December 9, 2024.   Public KB5005413: Mitigating NTLM Relay Attacks on Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS) Recommends the enablement of channel binding + Extended Protection for Authentication (EPA) in this case for AD CS but should be enabled for all services. Enabling EPA does not directly impact NTLM hash leak vulnerability but it impossible to use the hash leak in an NTLM relay attack.   NTLM section of Learn: Deprecated features for Windows client TalkingPoints: [2024-12-10] Talking Points for reported vulnerability with NTLM hash

symptom: Consider the following Scenario: An application is hosted on a network share and is launched on the RDS Session Host Server. Note: This applies to the applications hosted locally to the Session Host Server as well! You distribute the application to your end users either using a .rdp file or using RD Web Access Server When the end user launches the application following symptoms are seen:  The administrator can see the session hitting the Session Host server (verified using Task Manager) The instance of the application example abc.exe is launched within the session  After few seconds the session disconnects or may even get logged off!
rootCause: The defined code uses different timeouts one before the first app launch and one after the first app launches. This is because, even if we are actively configuring the system for the first login, there are instances when the initial server login can be sluggish when we start, which could lead to a lengthy period of invisible windows (empty session).We do occasionally see some temporary windows appear during the initial login, but there may still be times when the default of, say, 20 seconds passes without a window being seen.  In this RDS trace scenario we can see the logoff/disconnect happens before the first app launches
solution:
We collected RDS trace from the Session host server where the application was launched! RAIL.ETL should show you details on the application launch! We can see a graceful logoff/disconnect as the application fails to launch a) a visible window or b) a tooltip  In customer's scenario the issue was coming due to the network latency between the Session Host Server and the network share where the application was hosted.  But there could be other reasons like Server Sluggishness, Application behavior, some other process blocking the launch or network issues if the app hosted on a share or app uses data hosted remotely In this case, we asked customer to reduce the latency between the session host and the network where app is hosted to minimum which made the application launch! symptom: AbstractCurrent versions of Windows include the open-source tool OpenSSH. The Microsoft binary is a fork of OpenSSH: Release Notes, that includes some Windows specific compatibility changes. Major changes get pulled in from OpenBSD's OpenSSH-Portable. On February 19, 2025, the Qualys Threat Research Unit (TRU) identified two vulnerabilities in OpenSSH. CVE-2025-26465 allows an active machine-in-the-middle attack (MITM) on the OpenSSH client when the VerifyHostKeyDNS option is enabled. CVE-2025-26466 affects both the OpenSSH client and server, enabling a pre-authentication denial-of-service attack.   Both CVEs were mitigated per the OpenSSH Security page on February 18, 2025: ssh(1) in OpenSSH versions 6.8p1 to 9.9p1 (inclusive). VerifyHostKeyDNS server impersonation.    A logic error in ssh(1) allowed an on-path attacker to impersonate any server when the VerifyHostKeyDNS option is enabled. This option is disabled by default. This vulnerability has been assigned CVE-2025-26465.  For more information, please refer to the release notes and the report from the Qualys Security Advisory Team who discovered the bug. sshd(8) in OpenSSH versions 9.5p1 to 9.9p1 (inclusive). Denial of service in sshd(8). sshd(8) was subject to a pre-authentication memory/CPU denial-of-service attack using SSH2_MSG_PING packets. This attack may be mitigated using the existing PerSourcePenalties option. This vulnerability has been assigned CVE-2025-26466.For more information, please refer to the release notes and the report from the Qualys Security Advisory Team who discovered the bug.   The Microsoft Windows Development team is fully aware of (1.) CVE-2025-26465 and CVE-2025-26466 AND (2.) the release of updated versions of OpenSSH by OpenSSH.COM on February 18,2025. The Microsoft MSRC team has reviewed both CVEs and assigned each a medium severity / CVSS score. By policy and to protect customer data, Microsoft does NOT release status updates or external communications for CVEs with low or medium CVSS scores prior to the release of a mitigating fix. Unlike CURL, OpenSSH can be uninstalled via optional features which fully removes OpenSSH binaries from the machine.  Updated versions of OpenSSH on OpenSSH.COM and other trusted locations CAN be installed in an alternate directory. Only Windows Updates can install a newer version of OpenSSH in the %systemroot%\system32 directory.    Status Updates November 11, 2025: Bug table in Resolution section updated based on insights from Task 59269173. Updated versions of OpenSSH are shipping in 12B.25 for multiple OS versions. August 1, 2025. Based on TaskGroup 56311847 being parented to 2508 CyberEO which does NOT operate though CD servicing and October 16, 2025 due date in the Servicing tab of OS Bug 56311863, it is believed that that an updated version of OpenSSH will ship for Win11 24H2 on 10D.25 and on Windows Server 2025 in 11B.25. The due date in the servicing tab indicates a release date of 2025.10.16. CSS is NOT aware of ANY bugs shipping updated versions of OpenSSH on OS versions prior to GE (i.e. WIn11 24H2 and Windows Server 2025).   March 7, 2025: Task Group 56311847 has been filed to drive the release of an updated version of OpenSSH. The ONLY OS getting an updated version of OpenSSH is GE / WIn11 24H2 / WS 2025. Customer sharable text added to Resolution section of this article. February 25, 2025: ICM created. This KB will be updated with customer sharable coms in the coming days. Until further notice, case owners should add related cases to ICM 600458334. February 24, 2025. KB created based on social media reports + enterprise customers opening assisted support cases to obtain an updated version of OpenSSH.    Resources BleepingCommunity: Microsoft testing fix for Windows 11 bug breaking SSH connections BornCity: Multiple vulnerabilities in OpenSSH (Feb. 2025) February 18, 2025: Security experts from Qualys TRU have come across two vulnerabilities in the OpenSSH software. An advisory for another vulnerability was also published on February 11, 2024. Affected by this vulnerability are OpenSSL 3.4, 3.3 and 3.2, although there are upgrades to newer OpenSSL versions.  Qualsys Community: Qualys TRU Discovers Two Vulnerabilities in OpenSSH: CVE-2025-26465 & CVE-2025-26466 February 19, 2025 Blog post identifying 2 vulnerabilities in OpenSSH  TheRegister: FreSSH bugs undiscovered for years threaten OpenSSH security Lists CVSS score of 6.8 out of 10 for CVE-2025-26465 and 5.9 out of 10 for CVE-2025-26466.  ICM 600458334: OpenSSH that shipped in Windows Updates on and after 10B.24 is vulnerable to MITM CVE-2025-26465 and pre-authentication DOS CVE CVE-2025-26466 Task Group 59787069: Customer Escalation: OpenSSH 9.5p1 Vulnerabilities Flagged by Third-Party Audit (ERNW)  Risk to Microsoft Security Trust Task Group 56311847: [MSRC 95188] OpenSSH for Windows - CVE-2025-26465 and CVE-2025-26466 - Internal Parent bug that considers this fix for 17 child OS versions from W2K8 SP2 to current OS versions. Some of those OS versions don't ship OpenSSH. SLAs reference release dates as late as October 2025. Release date: TBD.  Internal KB5067432: Windows: CURL: Security scanning apps incorrectly report Windows CURL as vulnerable to CVE-2025-5399: WebSocket endless loop Latest CVE that security scanning apps are reporting that Windows CURL as vulnerable as of July, August and September of CY2025.   FAQ Regarding the fix mentioned in the release notes on OpenSSH: Security, can Windows OpenSSH use the configuration VerifyHostKeyDNS and PerSourcePenalties to mitigate this vulnerability  A1: VerifyHostKeyDNS can be disabled (as is the default) to mitigate in the short-term. PerSourcePenalties is NOT available in v9.5 (was added in 9.8) and is NOT a mitigation for OpenSSH for Windows at this time (current as of March 7, 2025).

symptom: On the Domain Controller in Group Policy Management Console (gpmc.msc), attempting to edit a Group Policy Object results in the following error: Group Policy Error:Failed to open the Group Policy Object. You might not have the appropriate rights. Details:The specified network name is no longer available.
rootCause: The issue was caused by SMB2 being disabled on the Domain Controller. Below is the output of the Get-SMBServerConfiguration command: ======================================20250207 16:21:20.473 : Get-SmbServerConfiguration            ======================================EnableSMB2Protocol : False --> SMB2 Disabled.
solution:
Open PowerShell as an Administrator:
Ensure you have administrative privileges to execute the necessary commands.   Check the Current SMB Configuration:
Run the following command to verify the current SMB configuration: Look for the EnableSMB2Protocol setting. If it is set to False, SMB2 is disabled.   Enable SMB2 Protocol:
To enable SMB2, execute the following command:Set-SmbServerConfiguration -EnableSMB2Protocol $true   Verify SMB2 is Enabled:
Run the Get-SmbServerConfiguration command again to confirm that EnableSMB2Protocol is now set to True.Get-SmbServerConfiguration     After re-enabling SMB2, the SYSVOL and C$ shares became accessible locally on the Domain Controller.This also restored the ability to edit Group Policy Objects (GPOs) on the Domain Controller.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#200155

symptom: Abstract  This article documents an Sev 2 post-rel issue tracked by a Sev 2 ICM introduced by an MSRC change introduced by 3D.25 Windows Updates persisted by 4B.25. updates where Windows container groups fail to start due to a regression in the new kernel. Public text has been added for Server 2025, Server 2022, Server 2019, Server 2016 in  KB5057292: Windows Server containers for April 2025  That text says:  IMPORTANT We are aware that the WS2025 floating tags (for example, mcr.microsoft.com/windows/servercore:LTSC2025) are currently retrieving the 2025.03 B (March 2025) container images instead of the latest 2025.04 B (April 2025) images. We are actively working to resolve this behavior.To work around this issue, we recommend using explicit tagssuch as the KB number, build number, or SHA256 (for example, mcr.microsoft.com/windows/servercore:10.0.26100.3775)to pull the latest 2025.04 B container image.   If you are using Hyper-V isolated windows containers and a 4B based (April 2025 image) sandbox or container image is being used with the other on a different version, you could see the following known errors while starting up the containers:  "guest RPC failure: The virtual machine or container exited unexpectedly.: unknown"  "failed to start containerized task"   Status Updates Resources  Outage 616931663: Regression when using 4B based images for hyperv isolated containers Incident 616415757: [ACI] HighApplicationDeploymentFailure more than 20 in WARP-Prod-LN over last 20mins Incident 616751621: Slow Gateway Startup Detected in ppvnet-westcentralus Bug 57091584: WSC.DEF HostFiles section is ignore in UtilityVM - Boards    Symptoms

rootCause: MSINTERNAL Root cause from ICM 545458952: [LSI#545565893] found netjoin errors from server and VM lost connection  A bug in the environment variable read code in JSON AD caused the 'ConfigSequenceNumber' environment variable read error, leading to the re-execution of the sequence number 0  MSINTERNAL Root cause from ICM 545565893: Global | Domain got changed automatically by extensions   Impacted subscriptions are typically (1.) long living VMs (2.) whose domain join password has expired OR (3.) the user account that is used to join the domain has expired among other potential reasons at which point (4.) version 1.3.13 re-executes the last sequence number and (5. ) it unjoins and rejoins the domain (6.) preventing VMs to from rejoin the domain they were previously joined to.   VMs that don't have such issues (password expiry, user account expiry, inability reach out to the domain controller etc.) should not face issues in the re-join operation.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#195913

symptom:
Pre-Windows 10 2004 (20H1) build number 19041 print clients CANNOT remotely connect to 8D.25-patched Win11 24H2, 9B.25-patched WS 2025 or 9D.25-patched Win11 25H2 print servers     Abstract   To meet security goals and support new print capabilities, the following Windows Updates transitioned the Windows printing components from MSVCRT to a modern Universal C Runtime Library.  KB 5065789: September 29, 2025KB5065789 (OS Builds 26200.6725 and 26100.6725) Preview              <- The 8D.25 Preview for WIn11 24H2 print servers KB 5065426: September 9, 2025KB5065426 (OS Build 26100.6584)                                                          <- The 9B.25 WU for WIn11 24H2 & WS 2025 print servers KB 5065789: September 29, 2025KB5065789 (OS Builds 26200.6725 and 26100.6725) Preview               <- the 9D.25 WU KB for Windows 11 25H2    As a result of this change, print clients prior to pre-Windows 10 2004 (20H1) build number 19041 regardless of patching level will intentionally fail to print to remote Windows 11 24H, Windows Server 2025 and Windows 11 25H2 print servers that have installed such updates with the following error:  The printer driver is not installed on this computer. Some printer properties will not be accessible unless you install the print driver.OR Windows cannot connect to the printer.OR Microsoft-Windows-PrintService event 808 will be logged in the Microsoft-Windows-PrintService\Admin event log of unsupported Windows print clients regardless of their patching level.     Workaround: (1.) Upgrade print clients to Windows 10 22H2 or newer OR (2.) configure print clients released prior to Windows 10 22H2 to use pre-Windows Server 2025 print servers. The issue affects the following OS versions acting as "print clients" remotely connecting to patched, "Ge" and newer print servers:  Windows clients prior to Windows 10 2004 (20H1) build number 19041 Windows Server 2019 Windows Server 2016    Is | Is NOT context:  Print clients connecting to pre-Windows 11 24H2 and Windows Server 2025 remote print servers are NOT impacted by this hardening change.  Print failures occur regardless of the patching level on Windows print clients. Local printing is NOT impacted by this hardening change.  Post GE print servers including Windows 11 25H2 also got this hardening change.  The primary impacted driver here is unidrv* but this change could also impact postscript and ps5ui as well.  There is NO / Zero containment (i.e. KIR) for this security hardening change to the Windows print components.      Status Updates  December 16, 2025:  Bug table for long-term fix by OS Bug 59820257 allowing WS 2019 and WS 2016 print clients to print to 9B.25-patched WS 2025 print servers added to Resolution59820257.  October 14, 2025. Link to public facing coms added to "Resources". Since the new behavior is intentional / by design, it is NOT documented within the WU Release notes for Windows 11 25H2 (which is based on 9B.25-patched Windows 24H2).  October 13, 2025: KB updated. Release note text requested for the 8D.25 WU KB for Windows 11 24H2, the 9B.25 WU KB for WS 2025 and the 9D.25 WU KB for Win11 25H2.  October 9, 2025: Internal KB created on behalf of cases opened against CSS.    Resources  Public facing coms:  Windows Server 2025: September
     9, 2025KB5065426 (OS Build 26100.6584) - Microsoft Support Windows 11, version 24H2: August
     29, 2025KB5064081 (OS Build 26100.5074) Preview - Microsoft Support        Symptoms  Pre-Windows 10 2004 (20H1) build number 19041 remotely connecting to ~ 9B.25-patched Win11 24H2, WS 2025 and Win11 25H2 print server may frequently encounter the following errors:The printer driver is not installed on this computer. Some printer properties will not be accessible unless you install the print driver.

ORWindows cannot connect to the printer. Microsoft-Windows-PrintService event 808 is logged in the Microsoft-Windows-PrintService/Admin event log. Log Name:      Microsoft-Windows-PrintService/AdminSource:        Microsoft-Windows-PrintServiceDate:          10/9/2025 5:02:02 PMEvent ID:      808Task Category: InitializingLevel:         ErrorKeywords:      Print SpoolerUser:          SYSTEMComputer:      <ServerName>Description:The print spooler failed to load a plug-in module C:\Windows\system32\spool\DRIVERS\x64\3\UNIDRVUI.DLL, error code 0x7F. See the event user data for context information. The following is logged[1]0658.13DC::10/09/25-19:47:36.8404230 [Admin ] The print spooler failed to load a plug-in module C:\Windows\system32\spool\DRIVERS\x64\3\UNIDRVUI.DLL, error code 0x7F. See the event user data for context information. C:\Windows\system32\spool\DRIVERS\x64\3\UNIDRVUI.DLL    Repro / No Repro environments 2016 Pre-September CU with 2019 Pre-September CU 2016 Pre-September CU with 2019 @September CU 2016 pre-September CU with 2025 Pre-September CU 2016 @September CU With 2025 Pre-September CU 2016 @September CU with 2025 @September CU <<< Repro Print server 2025 - 7b.25  no repro Print server 2025 - 8b.25  no repro Print server 2025  9b.25  repro 10.0.26100.6584
rootCause: In the customer environment that exposed this issue, Windows Server 2016 print clients connect to Windows 11 24H2 print servers that have the September 9B update (KB5065426) installed. After establishing the connection, the following files inC:\Windows\System32\spool\drivers\x64\3are updated to version 10.0.26100.6584: UNIDRV.DLL UNIDRVUI.DLL UNIRES.DLL  When the Print Spooler or PrintIsolationHost.exe attempts to load these updated DLLs, the process encounters LdrpReportError, resulting in printing failures. Microsoft internal answer  As part of the CyberEO and SFI initiative, Windows Updates released between 8D.25 and 9D.25 depending on OS version took a hardening change made the following change Print components were changed to build using UCRT instead of Vista era / legacy MSVCRT  The down-level support target for Print components was changed from VISTA to Windows 10 Vb.   The change was necessary to both harden the Windows print subsystem and add new capabilities. With Windows printing in a client/server scenario, the print server ensures that the drivers between it and the client are in sync. UNIDRV* is a core driver file that print server sends to the print client machine so that both machines use the same driver files. 9B.25-patched GE print servers use a new UNIDRV* binaries built with UCRT that can ONLY get Windows 10 Vb and newer print clients.Windows 10 Vb and newer introduced support for FrameHandler4 and with the build configuration, it is present in the UNIDRV* binaries on WS 2025. Windows Server 2019 and WS 2016 ALSO support UCRT but do NOT support FrameHandler4 Point: Pre-VB print clients fail to load the UCRT binary.
